primary_legislation.all: Prevention of Damage by Pests Act 1949; Health and Safety at Work Act 1974; Food Safety Act 1990 (staff kitchen areas); Environmental Protection Act 1990

enforcing_body.northern_ireland: Local Authority / District Council Environmental Health + RQIA

notes: Steps are identical to school MOD-013. Responsible person updated to Practice Manager. Pest control is particularly important in GP practices given medicines storage (dispensing practices — rodents in dispensary present a significant risk) and clinical waste storage areas.

logbooks.title: Quarterly Pest Control Inspection
logbooks.frequency_detail: Every 3 months by a professional pest control contractor (BPCA or RSPH qualified). Dispensing practices should consider a higher frequency (monthly) given the risk of rodent access to medicines storage.

steps.description: Arrange quarterly visit with a professional pest control contractor (BPCA-registered or equivalent). Confirm they hold relevant insurance and are trained in use of rodenticides.

Contractor inspects all areas: dispensary and medicines storage (dispensing practices — check for rodent evidence: droppings, gnaw marks, nesting material), clinical waste storage area (external), staff kitchen and food storage, bin store.
Contractor checks and services all bait stations and rodent traps: replenish bait, record activity, replace damaged traps.

Any evidence of active infestation must be escalated immediately. Affected areas (particularly medicines storage) must be assessed for contamination risk — contaminated medicines must be quarantined and not dispensed pending investigation.
steps.photo_guidance: Photograph any evidence of pest activity or damaged bait stations.

steps.description: Contractor provides a written service report. Record: date, areas inspected, pest activity observed (yes/no), bait replenished, any recommendations, any immediate actions required, next visit date.
